steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Heller's hypothesis says watercourts were designed to foster conch. We need the finding that most directly shows the watercourts could not have served that purpose.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Samples of animal remains collected at Calusa sites reflect a greater diversity of marine species, particularly among sea snails, within the perimeter of watercourts than in locations known to have been devoted to the preparation and consumption of food.’: This would support the hypothesis, not weaken it. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘Historical population-size estimates suggest that the sea snails and fish most common in the Calusa diet were plentiful in open coastal waters when the watercourts were constructed but decreased in abundance in the years immediately after construction.’: Addresses what was available, not whether the design suits conch. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘Radar surveys of Calusa sites reveal watercourt dimensions suitable for sustaining fish of many local species but not conducive for maintaining the shallow environments with ample seagrasses that allow sea snails to thrive.’: This shows the physical design itself cannot support the habitat snails need, directly contradicting the hypothesis. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘Sediment layers excavated from Calusa watercourt sites contain heterogeneous mixtures of scales from multiple species of fish and fragments of shells from various types of sea snails, but conch shells do not constitute the majority of the mixture in most of those layers.’: Shows low frequency but not that the design is unsuitable. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
